lesson.subtitle: Lesson 6 -- Understanding Different Accents
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 6 -- Understanding Different Accents

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: the way people from a country or region pronounce a language
the way the voice rises and falls when we speak
the part of a word we say with more force
the speed at which someone speaks
to make something clearer or easier to understand
to say the same idea again using different words
to hear and understand what someone says
a small sound or word, like um, used while thinking

reading.rtitle: A Call with the Bangalore Team
paras: Last week, Ana joined a video call with Rahul, a developer on the Bangalore team. Rahul speaks English every day at work, with an Indian accent, a natural intonation, and his own pace. At first, Ana missed a few words. The line was a little unclear, and Rahul spoke quickly. But Ana did not panic. She knew that a new accent is only new for a short time; after a few minutes, the ear adjusts.

Ana used simple listening strategies. First, she listened for the main idea and did not stop at every single word. When she misheard a detail, she used the context to guess it. And when she was still not sure, she asked a clarifying question. She said, 'Sorry, could you say that again, a little slower?' and 'What do you mean by staging?' She also confirmed the important points: 'So you are saying the test failed twice, right?' Rahul was happy to rephrase; good colleagues always are.

To check understanding, we use clarifying questions. Use 'Could you...?' for a polite request: Could you repeat that? Could you spell it? Use 'What do you mean by...?' to ask about one word or idea. Use 'So you are saying (that)...?' to confirm what you heard. These questions are normal and professional; asking them shows that you are listening carefully, not that your English is weak.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B2 reading, adapted from a real remote QA call

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The text explains why Indian English is wrong and hard to understand.
Ana used listening strategies and clarifying questions to follow a colleague with a different accent; clarifying is a normal, professional skill.
The text is a list of the developers on the Bangalore team.

items: Ana panicked when she could not understand every single word.
The text says the opposite: 'But Ana did not panic.'
Ana listened for the main idea first, not every single word.
The text says: 'she listened for the main idea and did not stop at every single word.'
According to the text, asking a clarifying question shows your English is weak.
The text says clarifying questions show you are listening carefully, not that your English is weak.
Rahul refused to repeat or rephrase anything for Ana.
The text says: 'Rahul was happy to rephrase; good colleagues always are.'

gapfill.text: Remember: ask 'Could you...?' for a polite request, 'What do you mean by...?' for one word or idea, and 'So you are saying...?' to confirm. A clarifying question is professional, not weak.

bank.label: Useful sentences to clarify and confirm
items: Sorry, could you say that again, a little slower?
What do you mean by staging?
So you are saying the test failed twice, right?
Could you spell that name for me, please?
I did not catch the last part. Could you repeat it?
Let me confirm: the deadline is Friday, correct?
Do you mean the old page or the new one?
I think the line is unclear. Could you say it once more?

lf.title: Which clarifying question fits?
items: Sorry, I did not catch that.

lf.text: Use 'Could you...?' for a polite request, 'What do you mean by...?' to ask about one word, and 'So you are saying...?' to confirm what you heard.

items.situation: Your colleague says a word you did not catch, and quite fast. Ask them to repeat it more slowly.
tips: Sorry, could you say that again, a little slower?
I did not catch that. Could you repeat it, please?

items.situation: You are not sure what 'staging' means in the sentence. Ask about that one word.
tips: What do you mean by staging?
Sorry, what does staging mean here?

items.situation: You want to confirm a detail you heard: the test failed twice. Confirm it in one sentence.
tips: So you are saying the test failed twice, right?
Let me confirm: the test failed twice, correct?

items.situation: The line is unclear and you missed a name. Ask them to spell it.
tips: Could you spell that name for me, please?
Sorry, the line is unclear. Could you spell it?

items: A colleague gives you three quick updates. After each one, ask one clarifying or confirming question. Use your notes.
Listen again and this time confirm every number and name you hear: 'So you are saying...?'
Summarize the update in your own words, then ask one final clarifying question. No notes.
The speaker talks fast and the line is unclear. Stay calm and say: 'Sorry, could you slow down a little? I want to get every detail.'

answerkey.title: Reveal the clarifying toolkit
list: Ask to repeat or slow down: 'Sorry, could you say that again, a little slower?'
Ask about one word: 'What do you mean by staging?'
Confirm what you heard: 'So you are saying the test failed twice, right?'
Ask to spell a detail: 'Could you spell that name for me, please?'
Strategy: listen for the main idea first, use the context, then clarify only what you still miss.
answerkey.note: A clarifying question is normal and professional. It shows you are listening carefully, not that your English is weak.

listenings.text: Hi Ana, this is Rahul from the Bangalore team. Thanks for the call earlier. I want to leave a quick summary, so nothing is lost. This afternoon, we deployed the new build to the staging server, and the login page is working well now. But the checkout still shows one bug on slow connections. The test failed twice this morning, so please do not move it to production yet. One more thing: if my accent is fast on the call, just stop me and ask me to repeat. I really do not mind. Can you confirm the plan by end of day? Thanks a lot, and talk soon.

Hi Ana, it is Priya, your team lead. Here are three quick tips for calls with any accent. First, do not try to catch every single word. Listen for the main idea, and let the small words go. Second, use the context. If you miss one word, the sentence around it usually tells you the meaning. Third, and most important, just ask. A clarifying question is not a weakness; it is a professional skill. Say 'Could you repeat that?' or 'What do you mean by that word?' Everyone does it, even native speakers. One last thing: after a few minutes, your ear adjusts to a new accent, so give it time. You are doing great.

extra_audio.text: First, Ana joined the video call with Rahul from the Bangalore team. Then she listened for the main idea and did not stop at every word. Next, when she misheard a detail, she used the context to guess it. After that, she asked a clarifying question and asked Rahul to slow down. Finally, she confirmed the important points before the call ended.
